critic_raw:
This multimodal vs. text-only sentiment analysis experiment for e-commerce customer satisfaction has several methodological problems.

1. RANDOM STRATIFIED SPLIT ON TEMPORAL E-COMMERCE DATA IGNORES PRODUCT LIFECYCLE DYNAMICS. The design uses a random split across 24 months of customer reviews. E-commerce review data is highly temporally structured: product launches, seasonal demand spikes, supply chain disruptions, and promotional campaigns create temporal clusters in both review sentiment and product imagery. A random split will mix reviews from promotional periods across train and test sets. The multimodal model — which receives promotional flags and images — will see promotional-period image patterns in training and can directly memorize the promotional-period satisfaction boost as an image style correlation. This cannot be detected without a temporal holdout.

2. PREPROCESSING FITTED ON ENTIRE DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING. The design explicitly states: 'Preprocessing ensures consistency by fitting on the entire dataset before splitting.' This applies to BERT tokenization, image normalization (ResNet mean and std), and metadata encoding. For a live e-commerce platform, new products and image styles appear over time. Fitting preprocessing on the full dataset ensures that the test set's image normalization anchors are computed using test-period product imagery — embedding test-period product portfolio information into the training normalization parameters.

3. IMAGENET-PRETRAINED RESNET-50 ON PRODUCT IMAGES IS AN UNVERIFIED FEATURE QUALITY ASSUMPTION. The design assumes that ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet will produce meaningful representations for product images. While product images are more similar to ImageNet than medical images, product image quality (professional photography vs. user-generated content) varies enormously in e-commerce. For customer reviews with attached images, a substantial fraction will be low-quality photos taken on mobile devices with poor lighting. The design does not address whether ResNet-50 features are informative for low-quality user-generated images, which is the actual deployment distribution.

4. THE SATISFACTION TARGET IS CONTINUOUS BUT THE SPLIT STRATEGY REFERENCES CLASS BALANCE. The design states it uses 'stratified random split across all available data months' to 'control for class imbalance' and 'preserve the distribution of satisfaction scores.' A continuous regression target has no class imbalance — stratification for regression must use binned quantiles rather than classes. The design's claim to 'balance the distribution' is either incorrect (treating a continuous target as discrete) or underspecified (not stating the binning scheme used for stratification).

5. THE BASELINE (TEXT-ONLY) USES IDENTICAL BERT EMBEDDINGS, SO THE COMPARISON TESTS FEATURE CONCATENATION, NOT TEXT ADEQUACY. The baseline uses 'the identical architecture but without image and metadata branches. The text embedding branch is preserved unchanged.' Since both models use the same BERT text branch, the comparison tests whether adding image features and metadata to existing BERT embeddings improves prediction — not whether multimodal learning is better than text learning per se. This is a narrower and less interesting claim than the stated hypothesis.
all_issues_raised: Random split on 24-month temporal e-commerce data mixes promotional and seasonal periods across train/test, allowing the model to memorize promotional-image correlations
Preprocessing including BERT tokenization and ResNet image normalization fitted on entire dataset before splitting embeds test-period product portfolio statistics into training
ImageNet ResNet-50 feature quality on low-quality user-generated product photos is unverified and may not be informative
Stratification for class balance is incoherent for a continuous regression target; no binning scheme is specified
Baseline uses identical BERT text branch; comparison tests feature concatenation benefit, not multimodal vs. text-only learning
Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting leaks test-period language and image distribution into training parameters
Stratified random split on 24-month e-commerce data ignores temporal autocorrelation in customer satisfaction scores
Baseline is text-only rather than text-plus-metadata, not testing the strongest unimodal competitor
Post-purchase survey non-response bias truncates the satisfaction label distribution toward higher scores
ResNet-50 ImageNet features may not transfer to product photography; contribution vs. hand-crafted statistics is not ablated
Preprocessing fit on entire dataset before splitting leaks test-period image and text statistics into training normalization
Internal inconsistency between stated random split design and specific validation month range (months 17-20) implies an undescribed temporal split
Image modality likely has small effect size over text for satisfaction prediction; design may be underpowered to detect real multimodal advantage
MAE on high-satisfaction-biased distribution rewards mode-predicting models rather than discriminative models; secondary metrics are more appropriate primary metrics
